title: Solution Architect Workflow
description: A non-code-generating workflow for solution architecture. Runs a structured 5-checkpoint discussion (problem framing, approach selection, component design, integration strategy, final assembly) producing an architecture document with ADRs. Works standalone via /bober-architect or as an optional pipeline phase via runArchitect().

features.title: 5-Checkpoint Discussion Flow
features.description: The core interactive workflow. Five explicit checkpoints: (1) Problem Framing — sharp questions about the problem space, constraints, success criteria. (2) Approach Selection — 2-3 architectural approaches with tradeoff matrices, user picks. (3) Component Design — component breakdown with TypeScript interfaces, responsibilities, dependencies. (4) Integration Strategy — data flow, API contracts, external dependencies, risk mapping. (5) Final Assembly — complete architecture document with ADRs from all decisions made during the flow.
acceptanceCriteria: AC1: The agent definition defines exactly 5 checkpoints with explicit instructions for each. Each checkpoint has: purpose, questions to ask, output to produce, criteria for proceeding.
AC2: Checkpoint 1 (Problem Framing) produces a Problem Statement section. Questions are concise, direct, no filler — senior-grade.
AC3: Checkpoint 2 (Approach Selection) presents 2-3 approaches as a structured comparison (not paragraphs). Each approach has: 1-sentence description, pros, cons, best-for. User selects.
AC4: Checkpoint 3 (Component Design) produces component breakdown with TypeScript-style interfaces/signatures for key boundaries. Each component: responsibility (1 sentence), interface, dependencies.
AC5: Checkpoint 4 (Integration Strategy) maps data flow between components and identifies integration risks with severity.
AC6: Checkpoint 5 (Final Assembly) compiles all checkpoint outputs into a single architecture document with ADRs auto-generated from decisions made at each checkpoint.
AC7: In autonomous mode, the agent self-discusses at each checkpoint, making decisions with codebase evidence citations, and documents the reasoning.

features.title: Architecture Document Format
features.description: The output artifact: a structured markdown document designed for senior engineers. No filler, every section earns its space. Includes ADRs for each major decision made during the discussion flow.
acceptanceCriteria: AC1: Document sections: Executive Summary (3-5 sentences max), Problem Statement, System Overview, Component Breakdown, Data Model, API Contracts, Integration Strategy, Architecture Decision Records, Risk Assessment, Open Questions.

AC2: ADR format per decision: Decision (1 sentence), Context (why needed), Options Considered (table: option | tradeoff), Rationale (why this option), Consequences (what changes), Risk (what could break).
AC3: Architecture document is capped at 500 lines. If content exceeds this, the agent distills.
AC4: Component interfaces use TypeScript-style type signatures, not prose descriptions.
AC5: Risk Assessment uses severity levels (critical/high/medium/low) with concrete mitigation strategies, not generic advice.

features.title: Interactive Skill + Standalone Usage
features.description: The /bober-architect slash command. Orchestrates the 5-checkpoint flow interactively, spawning subagents as needed, saving artifacts to .bober/architecture/. Works as a standalone tool independent of the sprint pipeline.
acceptanceCriteria: AC1: .claude/commands/bober-architect.md exists with frontmatter (name, description, argument-hint).
AC2: The skill orchestrates 5 checkpoints. At each checkpoint, it presents the output and waits for user review before proceeding.
AC3: User can challenge, refine, or reject decisions at any checkpoint. The agent revises based on feedback.
AC4: Final architecture document is saved to .bober/architecture/<specId>-architecture.md.
AC5: ADRs are saved as separate files: .bober/architecture/<specId>-adr-<N>.md for each decision.
AC6: skills/bober.architect/SKILL.md exists for npm distribution.

features.title: Programmatic Module + Pipeline Integration
features.description: src/orchestrator/architect-agent.ts with runArchitect() that can be called programmatically. Wired into the pipeline as an optional phase between research and planning. Config option pipeline.architectPhase controls activation.
acceptanceCriteria: AC1: src/orchestrator/architect-agent.ts exports runArchitect(userPrompt, projectRoot, config, researchDoc?): Promise<ArchitectResult>.
AC2: ArchitectResult type contains: id, timestamp, document (markdown string), adrs (ADR[]), componentCount, decisionCount.
AC3: pipeline.ts calls runArchitect() between runResearch() and runPlanner() when pipeline.architectPhase is true.
AC4: Architecture document is passed to planner as context. It is NOT passed to generator or evaluator (context distillation).
AC5: PipelineSectionSchema includes architectPhase: z.boolean().default(false). Defaults to false for backward compatibility.
AC6: bober-run.md and bober-brownfield.md skills updated to include architect phase in orchestration flow.
AC7: Existing configs without architectPhase still parse successfully.

features.title: State Helpers + Distribution
features.description: State management for architecture artifacts, init command integration, progress tracking, and history events.
acceptanceCriteria: AC1: src/state/architect-state.ts exports saveArchitecture(), readArchitecture(), saveADR(), readADRs().
AC2: ensureBoberDir creates .bober/architecture/ subdirectory.
AC3: src/cli/commands/init.ts includes bober.architect in skillMap and bober-architect.md in agentFiles.
AC4: New history events: architect-started, architect-checkpoint, architect-completed.
AC5: Progress tracking shows architect phase status (complete/pending) alongside research/design/outline.

assumptions: The architect workflow produces documents only, never code. Code generation remains the generator's job.
In interactive mode, each checkpoint requires explicit user approval before proceeding.
In autonomous/pipeline mode, the architect self-discusses using codebase evidence and proceeds without user input.
Architecture Decision Records follow a lightweight format: Decision, Context, Options, Rationale, Consequences, Risk.
The architect agent uses the same tool set as the planner (Read, Grep, Glob, Bash) — read-only codebase access plus Write for saving artifacts.
Architecture documents are saved to .bober/architecture/ as a new artifact directory.
outOfScope: Visual diagram generation (Mermaid, PlantUML) — text descriptions only for now
Architecture fitness functions or automated architecture validation
Multi-project/monorepo architecture spanning multiple bober projects
Architecture versioning or diff tracking across revisions

techNotes.dataModel: ArchitectResult { id, timestamp, document, adrs: ADR[], components: Component[], decisions: Decision[] }. ADR { id, title, decision, context, options, rationale, consequences, risk }.
techNotes.existingPatterns: Follow research-agent.ts pattern for the programmatic module. Follow bober-research.md pattern for the skill. Follow bober-researcher.md pattern for the agent definition. State helpers follow research-state.ts pattern.

nonFunctionalRequirements.requirement: Architecture documents must be useful 6 months later. No jargon without definition, no references to 'the current approach' without specifying what it is.
nonFunctionalRequirements.verificationMethod: Review agent instructions for explicit prohibitions on vague references and temporal language.

nonFunctionalRequirements.requirement: Backward compatible. Existing bober.config.json files without architectPhase must parse and work without errors.
nonFunctionalRequirements.verificationMethod: Verify the field defaults to false in the schema and parse the project's own config.

nonFunctionalRequirements.requirement: Architecture document capped at 500 lines. ADRs capped at 50 lines each. Prevents context bloat when passed to planner.
nonFunctionalRequirements.verificationMethod: Verify size limits stated in agent definition.

metadata.riskNotes: The 5-checkpoint discussion flow is the novel piece. The agent definition quality determines whether this produces senior-grade output or generic filler. Heavy investment in prompt engineering for the agent definition is the primary risk factor.
